data.title: T-Mobile Q1 earnings on deck: What to expect
data.description:
T-Mobile US (TMUS) is set to post first-quarter earnings on Tuesday, after markets close.

During the earnings call, investors would like to hear more on German group Deutsche Telekom’s (DTEGY) reported deal with T-Mobile.

Deutsche Telekom is exploring a full combination with T-Mobile to create a telecoms giant in what could be the largest ever public merger, Reuters reported in April, citing two people familiar ‌with the matter. Deliberations are said to be at a preliminary stage and any deal would require political support to move forward, according to a Bloomberg report.

Wall Street expects the telecom to post EPS of $2.11 on revenue of $23.02B during the quarter. Over the last one year, TMUS has beaten EPS estimates 100% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 75% of the time.

Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street are  bullish and rated the stock a Buy, while Seeking Alpha’s Quant ratings consider it a Hold.

T-Mobile is seeing strong demand for its broadband offerings, with its premium plans for streaming bundle subscription that includes Netflix, Hulu and Apple attracting customers. However, when it comes to wireless subscribers, the company is facing increased competition from rivals that are going for deals and offers to lure customers in.

“We think there are more upside levers versus downside risks,” says KeyBanc’s Brandon Nispel, including organic EBITDA growth acceleration, an advantageous network position, and a balance sheet that is “positioned for optimal optionality.”

“We expect Q1 2026 results to serve as our first catalyst, as we see a beat/raise coming,” Nispel added.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen no upward revisions, compared to three downward revisions, revenue estimates have been revised upward six times versus three downward moves.

The stock has lost nearly 7% so far this year, compared to the over 4% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

Earlier in the day, Verizon posted first-quarter results that topped profit expectations and delivered a milestone in wireless subscriber growth.

data.title: PayPal introduces identifier solution to improve ad targeting
data.description:
PayPal Holdings (PYPL) introduced PayPal Ads ID, an identifier based on verified commerce relationships, to enable more precise targeting of ads, the company said Monday.

By contrast, most identity solutions in advertising are based on probabilistic signals, it said. "Because it is verified at the point of purchase, PayPal Ads ID is deterministic rather than probabilistic and consistent across browsers, devices, and sessions," the company said in a press release.

The new identifier constructs aggregated, consented signals from browsing behavior and across the PayPal ecosystem. "When your identity layer is built on verified commerce relationships, you're no longer estimating who someone is, you're reaching them with confidence," said Mark Grether, senior vice president and general manager of PayPal Ads.

PayPal Ads ID is also designed to protect user privacy, the company said. The identifier is individually encrypted, aggregated, and deidentified to guard user privacy, merchant names, and transaction details, as PayPal does not share that information.

Its first technology partners testing the solution include Magnite, PubMatic, Rokt, and Taboola, which are integrating PayPal Ads ID across commerce, open web, CTV, and native environments.

PayPal (PYPL) stock fell 0.9% in Monday midday trading.

data.title: Deferred Brent signals this oil shock has legs
data.description:
The longer-term impact of the Middle East war may be clearest in deferred oil futures, according to macro strategist Jim Bianco of Bianco Research.

In a post on X on Monday, Bianco pointed to the December 2026 Brent crude contract, which he said “made a new high today.” The contract held its uptrend and is now up more than 42% year to date, trading at nearly $86 on April 27, according to his chart.

Deferred futures focus on the market’s judgment on future supply conditions. A short-lived shock should fade most visibly in later-dated contracts; instead, the sustained move suggests traders are embedding a more durable war premium into the oil curve.

The move comes as the Strait of Hormuz—where about 25% to 30% of global oil passes -- remains effectively shut to international shipping. Iran reportedly gave the U.S. a new proposal to reopen the vital waterway and end the war, with nuclear negotiations postponed for a later stage.

Meanwhile, front-month Nymex crude for June delivery (CL1:COM) gained 2% Monday to $96.28 per barrel.

data.title: Salesforce sues Microsoft in UK over Teams 'bundling' - report
data.description:
Salesforce (CRM) and its workspace messaging app Slack have sued Microsoft (MSFT) at ​London's High Court, alleging anticompetitive ‌practices related to the messaging and video app Teams, Reuters reported.

On April 23, Slack Technologies and related companies filed the lawsuit​, which a Slack ​spokesperson said was "because Microsoft's practices harmed competition, ⁠using tying and bundling of Teams ​to limit customer choice," the report added.

Microsoft and Salesforce did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Slack had in 2020 complained to the European Commission, accusing ​Microsoft of bundling Teams with its ​Office product to gain an unfair advantage over competitors, the report noted.

In September 2025, the European Commission accepted commitments from Microsoft to address EU competition concerns related to the Teams app. The regulator said at the time that the commitments address its concerns related to the tying of Microsoft Teams to the company's productivity applications Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, included in Office 365 and Microsoft 365 suites for business customers. Under the commitments, Microsoft agreed to — make available versions of these suites without Teams and at a reduced price; and allow customers with long-term licenses to switch to suites without Teams.

Last week, the U.K.'s Competition Appeal Tribunal certified ​a ⁠mass lawsuit alleging Microsoft overcharged British businesses to use Windows Server software on cloud ⁠computing ​services provided by rivals.

data.title: Catalyst Pharmaceuticals jumps on report of interest from Angelini Pharma
data.description:
Catalyst Pharmaceuticals (CPRX) soared 19% after a report that Italian drugmaker Angelini Pharma is evaluating a potential purchase of the drugmaker.

Angelini is working with advisers as it evaluates a potential acquisition of Catalyst (CPRX), according to a Bloomberg report on Monday, which cited people familiar with the matter. Catalyst has a market cap of $3.3 billion.

Angelini has been looking at other possible acquisition candidates, and there's no certainty that deliberations will result in a deal, according to the report.

“The evaluation of growth opportunities is an integral part of the company’s strategy,” a representative for Angelini said in a statement to Bloomberg, declining to comment on specific targets. “Angelini Pharma has already stated on several occasions the intention to look at expanding the group in the external markets and will continue to pursue this strategy with the aim of strengthening the group’s positioning and growth, and ensuring the best solutions for patients.”

Catalyst declined to comment to Bloomberg.

data.title: Wall Street inches lower ahead of Big Tech earnings and Fed meeting
data.description:
Wall Street slips lower on Monday as investors brace for a pivotal week—eyeing Big Tech earnings, the upcoming Federal Reserve decision, and ongoing developments in the U.S.-Iran conflict.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was near even, while the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.2%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.1%. 

From a sector-by-sector stance, 4 of the 11 S&P sectors are in the green, with communication services at the top. Going the other way, consumer discretionary has been the weakest performer. 

Earnings remain in focus this week, with Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta (META) scheduled to report results on Wednesday, followed by Apple (AAPL) the next day. 

Meanwhile, Iran gave the U.S. a new proposal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and end the war, with nuclear negotiations postponed for a later stage, according to Axios. 

“This may be the most important week for markets so far this year, with big tech earnings and a key Federal Reserve meeting coming at a time when markets are trying to figure out their next step now that it seems like the Iran conflict is contained,” Glen Smith, chief investment officer of GDS Wealth Management, stated. 

Additionally, U.S. Treasury yields moved higher. The 2-year yield (US2Y) rose to 3.80%, while the 10-year yield (US10Y) climbed to 4.32%, and the 30-year yield (US30Y) advanced to 4.93%. 

As for stocks that were on the move, Micron Technology (MU) climbed 5.8%, while shares of Domino's Pizza (DPZ) dropped -10.1%.

data.title: Verizon eyes multi-billion dollar AI opportunity
data.description:
Shares of Verizon Communications (VZ) were bid higher and achieved their biggest one-day gain since January on Q1 results and upgraded guidance for 2026 that reflected better-than-expected postpaid subscriptions and profit for the reported quarter.

The telecommunications provider now expects total retail postpaid phone additions for the year to be at the upper end of its forecast of 750M to 1M, offsetting below consensus total revenue. In respect of a rapidly dynamic environment, Verizon’s (VZ) guidance reflects a “prudent view of competitive dynamics and the macroeconomic environment.”

During the company’s earnings call with analysts, CEO Dan Schulman emphasized positive churn metrics, which have “profoundly positive implications on the business model.”

In March, churn improved to below 85 basis points, a “significant improvement from Q4,” and evidence that Verizon’s efforts “are resonating with customers.” More importantly, lower churn means marketing dollars are working more efficiently, making the company less reliant on costly promotions for growth.

Furthermore, Verizon (VZ) will maintain a “strong and sustainable dividend” and remains committed to returning capital to shareholders. By the end of this year, Verizon (VZ) expects to have paid down nearly all of the debt it assumed from Frontier from its most recent acquisition.

By further reducing costs and increasing EBITDA, Verizon’s (VZ) CFO Tony Skiadas said the company will be able to “run a lot more efficiently, absorb the transitional year in service revenue, and return a significant amount of cash to shareholders.”

Later on the call, management disclosed that the company is in “deep discussions” with hyperscalers on fiber and 5G deals, which will enable Verizon (VZ) to expand further into software-defined networks, increased scalability, and employ 5G for AI infrastructure, a revenue opportunity that could be worth “multi-billions in revenue,” and an investment that is “absolutely essential to Verizon.” CEO Schulman acknowledged that the company is currently working closely with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) to facilitate the goal of using AI to create more operational efficiencies and improve customer satisfaction scores.

“I'm quite pleased with the amount of progress [on AI] we’ve made in such a short period of time,” Schulman noted on the call.

data.title: AbbVie submits application to FDA for subcutaneous Skyrizi
data.description:
• AbbVie has filed an application to the US FDA seeking approval of a version of Skyrizi (risankizumab) that is given subcutaneously, a more palatable way to receive the medication.
  • The application is supported by data from the phase 3 AFFIRM trial which enrolled patients with moderately to severely active Crohn's disease.
  • Subcutaneous maintenance dosing is every eight weeks.
  • In March, AbbVie released data from the study showing it met its co-primary endpoints, Crohn's Disease Activity Index (CDAI) clinical remission and endoscopic response, compared to placebo at week 12. 
  • The company said it is expecting approval of subcutaneous Skyrizi by the end of the year.

data.title: US Department of War adds more Google AI models to its secure platform
data.description:
Google's (GOOG)(GOOGL) Gemini for Government 3.1 Pro and 3.0 Flash models were added to the U.S. Department of War's GenAI.mil, its secure AI platform, the department announced today.

Shares of the tech giant had edged up nearly 2% by noon trading on Monday.

"These upgrades significantly reduce hallucinations, improve output quality, and accelerate productivity, giving warfighters more time to focus on mission‑critical tasks," the department posted on X today.

Google's Gemini for Government was the first AI model the platform added late last year. Gemini 3.1 Pro is expected to "improve deep research, vision, and reasoning capabilities for complex and analytical planning tasks." Meanwhile, Gemini 3.0 Flash delivers more rapid "processing, translation, and visual analysis to support operational workflows."

GenAI.mil is available to about 3M civilian and military personnel. 

Google has also reportedly been in talks with the U.S. Department of War to see if the Pentagon could utilize its models for "all lawful uses" after the government's falling out with Anthropic (ANTHRO).

OpenAI (OPENAI) has already secured a deal with the Department of Defense to deploy its models within a classified government network.

data.title: Domino's expects low-single-digit 2026 U.S. comps amid macro pressure while targeting 175-plus net U.S. stores
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Domino's Pizza, Inc. (DPZ) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Russell Weiner framed Q1 as share-gaining but below plan, saying, "Q1 represented another quarter of positive order count and market share growth for Domino's in the U.S." He added, "performance for the rest of the quarter did not meet our expectations, resulting in same-store sales of 0.9%."
  • Weiner tied the quarter’s deceleration to consumer and competitive dynamics, stating, "pressure intensified throughout the quarter, in particular, in March because of growing consumer uncertainty" and "competition within the QSR Pizza space also increased in Q1 as the national pizza players offered deals comparable, if not identical" to Domino’s value offers.
  • Weiner emphasized technology and operations as drivers of repeat behavior, highlighting, "We fully launched our new app" and that the updated Pizza Tracker "provides more precise ready time based on new AI technology." He also described the in-store DomOS orchestration agent, saying it can "alert a store to hold that order so it isn't made until a driver is there," with a goal of "just-in-time pizza making."
  • Weiner reiterated a key internal target despite the guidance revision, saying, "We are committed to doing everything we can to deliver 3% same-store sales in the U.S. for the year," and pointed to incremental plans: "starting as soon as May you're going to see things on the calendar or in media from Domino's that weren't on our calendar to start the year."
  • CFO Sandeep Reddy reported profit growth that excluded discrete items, stating, "Income from operations increased 4.2% in Q1, excluding the impact of foreign currency and a gain on the sale of the company's corporate aircraft." He added, "global retail sales grew 3.4% in the quarter" excluding FX, driven by "positive U.S. comps and global net store growth of more than 900 stores over the past 12 months."
  • Reddy detailed capital returns and authorization, saying, "Through April 21, we repurchased approximately 446,000 shares for a total of $170 million year-to-date" and "we had approximately $1.29 billion remaining on our share repurchase authorization," including "the additional $1 billion share repurchase authorization that the Board approved in April."

Outlook

  • Management updated full-year 2026 expectations (excluding the 53rd week). Reddy said, "we now expect our U.S. comp to be up low single digits in 2026" and "we now expect our international same-store sales growth to be low single digits."
  • Reddy maintained unit growth targets, stating, "we continue to expect 175-plus net stores in the U.S. and approximately 800 net stores in our international business."
  • Reddy revised the top-line growth framework tied to the comp reset, saying, "we now believe our global retail sales growth will be up mid-single digits for the year," and updated profit growth expectations: "we now expect operating income growth of mid- to high single digits" excluding FX, refranchising gains, and the aircraft sale gain.
  • Compared with the prior quarter, the U.S. comp outlook shifted from "3%" (Q4 call) to "up low single digits" (Q1 call). International expectations shifted from "1% to 2%" (Q4 call) to "low single digits" (Q1 call), with Reddy attributing the change to "macro and geopolitical uncertainty across the world."

Financial Results

  • Domino’s reported U.S. same-store sales growth of 0.9% in Q1, with Reddy attributing performance to "our marketing promotions and continued growth in our aggregator business" alongside "a challenging macro environment" and "increased competitive activity."
  • Reddy described comp drivers as "a balance of positive order counts and a positive average ticket," adding, "Ticket benefited from 0.9% of pricing, partially offset by a negative mix impact."
  • Channel performance diverged, with Reddy reporting, "Our carryout comps were up 2.4% and delivery was down 0.3%."
  • Store growth continued in the U.S., with Reddy saying, "We added 19 net new stores, bringing our U.S. system store count to more than 7,200." Internationally, he said retail sales grew "4%, excluding the impact of foreign currency" while same-store sales declined "0.4%."

Q&A

  • David Tarantino, Baird: asked why comps could stay positive into tougher comparisons and whether Domino’s would lean further into value; CEO Russell Weiner responded, "our objective continues to be for the year in the U.S., 3% same-store sales" and said plans changed: "our plans moving forward will look very different than they were starting the year."
  • Gregory Francfort, Guggenheim: asked about competitive rationality and whether closures could accelerate; Weiner replied, "to that, we say bring it on because we're built to do that stuff over time," and linked value matching to franchisee strain and "store closures" over time.
  • David Palmer, Evercore ISI: raised investor concern about pizza category growth and delivery competition; Weiner said category growth has been consistently "1% to 2%" and argued aggregators helped stabilize delivery: "this last quarter, on delivery, we held serve on total delivery."
  • Danilo Gargiulo, Bernstein: questioned why continue buybacks vs. reducing leverage given volatility; Reddy said leverage moved to "4.3" this quarter, and that the earlier "decision tree does not change," adding, "we will lean in, but with discipline."
  • Hyun Jin Cho, Goldman Sachs: asked about war impacts and the DPE turnaround; Weiner said Middle East franchisees "have not seen an impact from the war" and noted the region is "about 2% of our operating income," while adding DPE’s new CEO "starts in August" and Domino’s is "leaning in" to help fix value and order counts.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, repeatedly pressing on category growth durability, competitive intensity/value, consumer pressure, and capital returns vs. leverage (e.g., Palmer citing a "common investor view and pushback").
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks but more defensive-leaning in Q&A when addressing the miss and guidance reset, using clarifiers such as Weiner’s "those were reasons, not excuses" and Reddy’s emphasis, "I want to emphasize the positive."
  • Versus the prior quarter’s more celebratory posture (Q4: "deliver some of the best results within all of QSR"), Q1 messaging carried more urgency and adjustment language (Q1: "adjusting our plans in the second half of the year"), while maintaining confidence in the long-term framework (Reddy: "our belief in the long-term algorithm ... has not changed").

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language tightened: Q4 positioned a 2026 comp of "3%" with higher first-half timing; Q1 reset the formal outlook to "up low single digits" while Weiner reiterated the internal "objective" of 3%.
  • The narrative shifted from initiative momentum (Q4 highlighting Parmesan Stuffed Crust and Best Deal Ever) to macro/competitive pressure and calendar changes (Q1: "starting as soon as May" incremental marketing and innovation).
  • Analyst focus broadened from sustainability of delivery and share gains (Q4) to a heavier emphasis on consumer uncertainty, competitive discounting, and financial policy under volatility (Q1 leverage/buyback scrutiny).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "growing consumer uncertainty" with sentiment at "COVID-level lows" and "ongoing inflation" affecting purchase decisions.
  • Competitive intensity increased as rivals ran "deals comparable, if not identical" to Domino’s; Weiner characterized the near-term effect as "some short-term pressure" while arguing Domino’s "profit power" and advertising scale can withstand it.
  • International uncertainty was framed around "macro and geopolitical uncertainty across the world," with DPE singled out as a near-term execution focus while leadership transition timing was noted (new CEO "starts in August").

Final Takeaway

Domino’s management described Q1 as a share-gaining quarter that fell short of internal expectations, prompting a formal reset to low-single-digit same-store sales growth in the U.S. and internationally for 2026 while holding unit growth targets. Leadership emphasized rapid calendar and marketing adjustments beginning in May, stepped-up product innovation in the second half, continued technology upgrades (app, AI-enabled tracker, and DomOS), and an ongoing capital return posture supported by expanded repurchase authorization, while acknowledging a more pressured consumer and heightened promotional competition.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
